narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 64.6% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed strongly. The primary reason for this decision was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the typical average of around the 5th or 6th decile—and this stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure. Your LSAT score of 35, while respectable, fell short of the average 36.7 seen in successful applicants, acting as the second-most influential factor by nudging the odds slightly away from passing. Meanwhile, your middle family income bracket contributed modestly toward the failure prediction, as higher incomes in the dataset often correlate with better outcomes. Compared to typical students who pass, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 is also below the 3.21 average, though it played a lesser role here, and your full-time student status aligns with the vast majority who succeed. These elements combined to paint a riskier profile than the model favors for admission. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially given your dedication as a full-time student. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic decile through targeted study habits or coursework, perhaps retaking the LSAT to aim above the typical 37, and highlighting any extenuating circumstances around your Year 3 performance. Consider gaining practical experience, like internships, to build a more competitive narrative. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the model's view in your favor. Keep pushing forward—many overcome similar hurdles to succeed in law.
